entities.description: Hammond's direct base witness. This large parchment miscellany was produced in Leicester in the second half of the fifteenth century and is associated with John Shirley. The independent twenty-one-stanza Complaint occupies ff. 30va-31ra and has its own rubric and explicit.
entities.label: British Library Harley MS 7333

physicalDescription.format: Large codex, two columns, Gothic cursive
notes: The current catalog associates production with Leicester, John Shirley, and possibly St Mary de Pratis.

rights.basis: The anonymous medieval work and all surviving witnesses were created centuries before modern copyright. This does not govern modern photographs, transcriptions, editions, interfaces, or provider terms.
rights.component: Underlying medieval textual work and witness text

entities.description: A paper manuscript copied in London by the Hammond Scribe, perhaps John Multon, for John Vale. Its twenty-stanza Complaint on current ff. 271r-273r (medieval fols. 298-300) follows Chaucer's Complaint to His Purse without an independent break and was collated by Hammond.

reviewNotes: The primary transmission path is repository XML to U-M encoding to Hammond's 1909 Harley 7333 edition to Harley MS 7333; Harley 2251 and Add 34360 are collateral witnesses used in Hammond's collation.
The twenty-one-stanza, 147-line extent belongs to the Harley 7333 version encoded by CME00094. The two other witnesses and the later METS base text have twenty stanzas and 140 lines.
The later METS edition is supporting scholarship, not an upstream source of the 1909-based encoding.
The inherited Lydgate attribution in the Add 34360 catalog is not adopted; Hammond and the modern METS edition treat authorship as unknown.

No BL manuscript imagery is cached because all three legacy objects were unavailable at review time. The manifest records that dated service state, request routes, and whole-object reproduction preference without claiming the manuscripts were never digitized.
All public supporting files used were downloaded complete where possible and hard-linked into the main checkout's ignored source cache.

lineage.summary: CME00094 is the checked-in repository copy of the University of Michigan encoding of the twenty-one-stanza, 147-line version of the anonymous Complaint of a Prisoner against Fortune printed from British Library Harley MS 7333 by Eleanor Prescott Hammond in 1909. The encoding contains Hammond's poem on printed pp. 484-490 but not her introduction on pp. 481-483. Hammond transcribed Harley 7333, ff. 30va-31ra, and collated the related twenty-stanza copies in Harley MS 2251 and Add MS 34360. DIMEV v1.0 documents all three witnesses. A separate 2005 METS edition uses Harley 2251 as its base and is therefore supporting rather than upstream of CME00094. Complete copies of Anglia volume 32, DIMEV v1.0, the complete METS book PDF, and the current British Library digitisation price list are cached. The BL catalog's legacy digital-image links currently report the three manuscript objects unavailable, so no manuscript facsimile is claimed as downloaded.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Fifteenth century; surviving witnesses from the second half of the fifteenth century
catalogSummary.region: England; exact place of composition unknown
catalogSummary.title: Complaint of a Prisoner against Fortune

catalogingSubject.label: Complaint of a Prisoner against Fortune
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English rhyme-royal complaint represented by the complete twenty-one-stanza Harley MS 7333 version that Eleanor Prescott Hammond printed on pp. 484-490 and U-M encoded as CME00094. The two related copies in Harley MS 2251 and Add MS 34360 have twenty stanzas, and the later 2005 METS edition uses Harley 2251 as its base. Those versions, Hammond's full article, its digital scan, and the three physical codices are related layers rather than the abstract work itself.

assertion.notes: The METS record dates the work broadly to the fifteenth century. This does not convert any witness's copying date into an exact composition date.
The witness ranges overlap and the evidence does not establish a unique earliest copy, so no singular source_lineage_entity_id is assigned.

dateStatements.display: Second half of the fifteenth century (three surviving witnesses)

extent.description: One DIV1 poem with 21 LG stanza groups and 147 L verse lines, plus 22 HEAD, 54 NOTE, 29 MILESTONE, seven PB, and one P element. The counts describe the CME representation of the Harley 7333 version; the Harley 2251 and Add 34360 versions have twenty stanzas and 140 lines.

formStatements.description: The represented Harley 7333 version comprises twenty-one seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas (ababbcc), totaling 147 verse lines.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The brief embedded maxims do not make the poem mixed in textual form.

scope.description: An embedded-language facet for two Latin moral maxims within the otherwise Middle English dialogue

notes: The preferred title follows the modern METS edition and DIMEV; Hammond's 'Lament' is retained as the immediate edition's alternate title.
The inherited Lydgate attribution in the Add 34360 catalog is not accepted as authorship evidence. Hammond explicitly found no basis for it, and METS catalogs the author as unknown.
Harley 7333's Leicester production and the Hammond Scribe's London activity belong to witness history, not to the abstract work's unproved place of composition.
The two Latin maxims are embedded verse lines within a substantively Middle English poem and do not change the whole-work form from verse.
Italic spans preserve Hammond's printed editorial typography; they do not reveal the original manuscript abbreviation signs without access to manuscript images.

The complete Anglia volume, DIMEV v1.0 release, complete METS book PDF, and current BL digitisation price list are cached locally. No manuscript facsimile is cached because all three legacy BL image objects were unavailable at review time.

next_steps: Search modern work-specific bibliography beyond Hammond and the METS introduction.
Treat Lydgate, Thomas Usk, George Ashby, Sir Richard Roos, and William de la Pole only as rejected or unproved proposals.
Keep witness copying places distinct from composition place.
openQuestions.question: Can later scholarship identify the author or narrow the fifteenth-century date and English place of composition?

next_steps: Consult a modern collation or stemma of all three witnesses.
Compare the full Hammond and METS apparatuses while retaining their distinct base texts.
Do not infer a specific lost Shirley exemplar without direct evidence.
openQuestions.question: How does Harley 7333's additional stanza relate stemmatically to the twenty-stanza Harley 2251 and Add 34360 versions?

next_steps: Ask BL Reference Services whether the unavailable legacy ARKs can be restored.
If new imaging is needed, request each complete codex while separately mapping this work's folio range.
Ask for a shelfmark-specific quote and explicit Git, publication, and print-on-demand permissions before redistributing supplied images.
openQuestions.question: Can complete color facsimiles of Harley MSS 7333 and 2251 and Add MS 34360 be restored or supplied with written reuse terms?

assertion.notes: London copying for two witnesses and Leicester production for Harley 7333 are witness facts, not proof of the poem's place of composition.

scope.description: Cultural and linguistic origin of the surviving Middle English work

assertion.notes: Hammond found no reason for the inherited Lydgate attribution, and the modern METS edition lists the author as unknown. Comparisons with Thomas Usk, George Ashby, Sir Richard Roos, and William de la Pole do not establish authorship.

notes: This is editorial responsibility for the source edition, not medieval authorship.

scope.description: Editor of the 1909 immediate source representation
Collation of the Harley 7333 base against Harley 2251 and Add 34360

assertion.notes: The prisoner laments lost good name and abandonment by former friends.

summaries.value: An imprisoned speaker accuses Fortune of destroying his freedom, reputation, and friendships. Fortune answers that suffering follows sin, urges courage and patience, and redirects him from the pagan Fates toward God. The poem closes with reflection on punishment and Purgatory and with prayer to the Church, God, and the Virgin Mary. CME00094 represents the complete twenty-one-stanza Harley 7333 version; the two other surviving copies omit one stanza.

Twenty-one seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas in the represented Harley 7333 version. The complaint begins with the prisoner's address to Fortune, shifts to Fortune's reply, and returns to the prisoner's penitential and devotional conclusion.

work.summary: An imprisoned speaker accuses Fortune of destroying his freedom, reputation, and friendships. Fortune answers that suffering follows sin, urges courage and patience, and redirects him from the pagan Fates toward God. The poem closes with reflection on punishment and Purgatory and with prayer to the Church, God, and the Virgin Mary. CME00094 represents the complete twenty-one-stanza Harley 7333 version; the two other surviving copies omit one stanza.
